question: Why is the sky blue?
answer: Sunlight is a mix of all colours, but when it hits the molecules in our atmosphere (mostly nitrogen and oxygen) the shorter, bluer wavelengths get scattered in every direction far more than the longer, redder ones. This is called Rayleigh scattering. So when you look at the sky away from the Sun, what you see is sunlight that has been bounced sideways into your eyes — and that scattered light is dominated by blue. At sunset the Sun's light travels through much more atmosphere to reach you, so by the time it arrives, almost all the blue has been scattered away and only the reds and oranges are left.

question: Why can you see your breath on a cold day?
answer: The air you breathe out is warm and almost saturated with water vapour from your lungs. When that warm wet air hits cold outside air, it cools so fast that the air can no longer hold all the vapour — the extra water condenses into millions of tiny liquid droplets, which is exactly what a cloud is. You're literally breathing out a tiny cloud. On a warm day the same vapour comes out, but the surrounding air can still hold it as invisible gas, so nothing condenses and you see nothing.

question: Why does ice float, when almost every other solid sinks in its own liquid?
answer: Water is weird. Most substances pack tighter as they freeze, so the solid is denser than the liquid and sinks. But water molecules have a lopsided shape and form hydrogen bonds, and when water freezes those bonds lock the molecules into an open hexagonal cage with more empty space between them than in liquid water. So ice is about 9% less dense than liquid water (roughly 917 vs 1000 kg per cubic metre) and floats. This is the reason lakes freeze top-down rather than bottom-up — which is lucky for the fish underneath.

question: If you light a candle on the International Space Station, what shape is the flame?
answer: A small blue sphere. On Earth the familiar teardrop shape exists because hot air is less dense than cool air, so it rises — pulling fresh oxygen in from below and stretching the flame upward. In orbit everything is in free fall, so there is no 'up' for hot air to rise to. Convection stops. Oxygen can only reach the flame by slow diffusion from all sides, so the flame burns as a tiny ball, cooler and dimmer than on Earth, and blue because there is too little oxygen for the yellow soot glow we usually see.

question: A primary rainbow goes red-orange-yellow-green-blue-violet from outside to inside. Sometimes you can see a fainter second rainbow above it. What order are the colours in?
answer: Reversed — violet on the outside, red on the inside. A rainbow is sunlight that has entered a raindrop, bounced off the back, and come out the front, split into colours by refraction. The primary bow uses one internal bounce. The secondary bow is light that bounced twice inside the drop before coming out. That extra bounce flips the geometry, so the colour order is reversed, and because some light escapes at each bounce, the secondary bow is dimmer.

question: A hunter walks 1 km due south, then 1 km due east, then 1 km due north — and finds herself back where she started. What colour is the bear she sees there?
answer: White. To return to the start after walking south, east, and north for equal distances, she must have started at the North Pole: walking south takes her down a meridian, walking east takes her along a circle of latitude (which loops back on itself), and walking north brings her back up the same meridian to the Pole. The only bears living at the North Pole are polar bears. (Pedant's note: there are also rings near the South Pole where this works geometrically, but no bears live there.)

question: A wooden cube is painted red on all six faces, then sliced into 27 small identical cubes (3 along each edge). How many of the small cubes have paint on exactly two faces?
answer: 12. Think about where each kind of small cube lives. The 8 corner cubes have 3 painted faces. The cube at the centre of each large face (6 of them) has 1 painted face. The single cube buried in the middle has 0 painted faces. The cubes with exactly 2 painted faces are the ones sitting in the middle of each edge of the big cube — one per edge, and a cube has 12 edges, so the answer is 12.

question: An ant stands on one corner of a hollow 1-metre cube and wants to walk along the surface to the corner diagonally opposite. What is the shortest path?
answer: Unfold two adjacent faces of the cube flat onto a 2-by-1 rectangle and draw a straight line from one corner to the far corner — that line crosses the shared edge at its midpoint, and its length is sqrt(1^2 + 2^2) = sqrt(5), about 2.236 metres. The tempting answer of going along an edge then diagonally across one face gives 1 + sqrt(2) ~ 2.414 m, which is longer. The trick is that a straight line on the unfolded surface is still a straight line when you fold the cube back up.

question: Take a strip of paper, give one end a single half-twist, and tape the ends together to make a Mobius strip. Now cut it lengthways down the middle, all the way around. What do you get?
answer: Not two loops — one single longer loop with two full twists in it. Because a Mobius strip has only one side and one edge, your scissors never reach a 'second' piece to separate from. The cut just traces the one continuous edge and you end up with a single, thinner, longer band. If you cut that band down the middle again, then you get two separate loops — linked through each other.

question: A penny is dropped from the top of the Empire State Building (about 380 m). When it hits the ground, will it punch through the pavement, kill someone, or barely sting?
answer: It barely stings. A penny is light and flat, so air resistance catches up with gravity very quickly — it reaches terminal velocity (about 40 km/h, or 25 mph) after only about 15 m of fall. After that it doesn't speed up no matter how far it has left to drop, and it flutters down like a leaf. At 40 km/h a 2.5-gram penny carries far too little energy to do real damage; it would feel like being flicked. The 'penny from a skyscraper kills' story is a myth.

question: You're in a car holding the string of a helium balloon. The car accelerates forward hard. Which way does the balloon swing — back, or forward?
answer: Forward. When the car accelerates, everything that has mass (you, the air inside the car, your coffee) gets pushed backwards relative to the car by inertia. The air inside the car is heavier than helium, so the air piles up at the back and the pressure becomes higher at the back than at the front. The light helium balloon floats away from high pressure toward low pressure — exactly the way it floats up away from the denser air below it on the ground. Acceleration acts like a sideways gravity, and helium always rises against gravity.

question: Two trains are 100 km apart on the same track, heading toward each other at 50 km/h each. A fly starts on the nose of one train flying 75 km/h, bounces off the other train, comes back, bounces off the first train, and keeps zigzagging until the trains meet. How far does the fly fly in total?
answer: 75 km. The hard way is to add up the infinite series of shrinking back-and-forth trips. The easy way: the trains close the 100 km gap at a combined 100 km/h, so they meet in exactly 1 hour. The fly was in the air, flying at 75 km/h, for that whole hour. 75 km/h x 1 h = 75 km. (Famously, when this was posed to John von Neumann he answered instantly. The questioner said 'ah, you saw the trick' — von Neumann said 'what trick? I just summed the series.')

question: You have one raw egg and one hard-boiled egg, identical from the outside. Without cracking them, how can you tell which is which?
answer: Spin them on a flat surface. The hard-boiled egg is solid all the way through — shell and insides spin together as one rigid body, so it spins fast and smoothly. The raw egg has a liquid yolk and white inside; when you spin the shell, the liquid lags behind due to its inertia, sloshes around, and shifts the centre of mass, so it wobbles and spins much more slowly. Bonus test: briefly stop each egg with a fingertip and let go. The boiled egg stays stopped. The raw egg starts moving again on its own — the liquid inside was still spinning even after you stopped the shell.

question: A standard soccer ball is stitched from black pentagons and white hexagons. There are 20 hexagons. How many pentagons must there be?
answer: Exactly 12, and it has to be 12 — no choice. A soccer ball is a truncated icosahedron, a convex polyhedron, so it obeys Euler's formula V - E + F = 2. Count carefully: every pentagon edge is shared with a hexagon, and every vertex is the meeting of two hexagons and one pentagon. Working through the algebra, the hexagons cancel out and you're forced to have 12 pentagons regardless of how many hexagons you add. That's why every soccer ball, every fullerene molecule (C60), and every geodesic dome built this way has exactly 12 pentagonal panels.

question: An ant at the bottom corner of a tin can wants to walk on the outside surface to the point diametrically opposite at the top. The can is 10 cm tall and 10 cm in circumference. What's the shortest path, and how long is it?
answer: About 11.18 cm. Cut the can open along the vertical line through the starting corner and unroll it flat — the curved side becomes a 10-cm-tall, 10-cm-wide rectangle. The ant's target sits halfway across (5 cm) and at the top (10 cm). A straight line on the flat sheet is sqrt(5^2 + 10^2) = sqrt(125) ~ 11.18 cm. When you roll the rectangle back into a cylinder, that straight line becomes a graceful spiral on the surface — and it's still the shortest route, because a cylinder is intrinsically flat (zero Gaussian curvature) and shortest lines on flat surfaces are straight lines.
